subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Fits an introductory Psychology 11/12 lesson by establishing psychology as the scientific study of thought, feeling, action, evidence, method, and explanation.
unit_context: Cycle A, Lesson 1. This lesson starts the foundations cycle by building shared language for evidence-based interpretation.
assignment_purpose: Students practise making a careful interpretation from limited evidence, then revise the interpretation by naming what additional evidence would be needed.
final_evidence_target: A short written interpretation that defines psychology, separates internal experience from observable action, and explains why evidence and limits matter.

student_task_flow.label: Part 1
student_task_flow.evidence: Students define psychology using science, thought, feeling, and action.

student_task_flow.label: Part 2
student_task_flow.evidence: Students compare internal experience examples with observable action examples.

student_task_flow.label: Part 3
student_task_flow.evidence: Students improve an interpretation by adding evidence and limits.

success_criteria: I can define psychology as a science.
I can separate an internal experience from an observable action.
I can use evidence before making an interpretation.
I can explain what the evidence does not show yet.
supports_scaffolds: Sentence frame: One possible interpretation is...
Sentence frame: The evidence I have is...
Sentence frame: The evidence does not show...
Board anchor: claim, evidence, method, limits
differentiation_model: All students work toward the same explanation goal. Students needing support use sentence frames and example sorting. Students ready for extension add a second possible interpretation and name a better method for testing it.
checkpoint_release_logic: Teacher checks Part 2 before students complete Part 3. Students must show one internal example and one observable action example before revising the interpretation.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep the focus on evidence and interpretation rather than personal comments about classmates.
Use fictional or general examples so the lesson stays classroom-safe.
Ask students to name what they observed before they explain it.
Require students to include one limit in the final response.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think psychology is mainly opinion rather than evidence-based study.
Students may treat one example as enough evidence for a broad conclusion.
Students may confuse a testable prediction with a broad theory.
Students may forget to name limits when explaining action.
pacing_shape: Single 50-minute lesson: short launch, direct vocabulary build, guided comparison, independent interpretation revision, exit response.
assessment_focus: Clear separation of internal experience and observable action

teacher_guide.title: Cycle A Lesson 1 Teacher Guide — What Is Psychology?
teacher_guide.overview: This lesson introduces psychology as a science that studies thought, feeling, and action through evidence-based interpretation. Students practise moving from observation to evidence to explanation while keeping limits visible.
teacher_guide.essential_question: How can psychology help us explain human action more carefully?
teacher_guide.big_idea: A strong psychology explanation is not just a confident answer. It connects a claim to evidence, uses a reasonable method, and names what the evidence cannot prove yet.
teacher_guide.opening_frame: Tell students that this course will ask them to slow down explanations. The habit is simple: describe what was observed, identify possible evidence, consider a method, and name limits before making a broad interpretation.
teacher_guide.frame_line: Use this repeated language throughout the lesson: claim, evidence, method, limits. Keep these four words visible during the task sheet and exit response.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: The exit response should show that students can define psychology, compare internal experience with observable action, and improve an interpretation by adding evidence and limits.
learning_goals: Students define psychology as a science of thought, feeling, and action.
Students compare internal experience examples with observable action examples.
Students explain why evidence is needed before making an interpretation.
Students revise a weak interpretation by adding evidence, alternatives, and limits.
materials: Board anchor with four words: claim, evidence, method, limits
integrity_checks: Use fictional or general examples only.
Keep student-facing language focused on actions and evidence.
Do not let one observation become a broad conclusion.
Require a limit statement before accepting the exit response as complete.
teacher_notes: If students jump straight to explanation, ask what they actually observed.
If students give only a definition, ask them to apply it to the comparison example.
If students give only one possible explanation, ask what else could fit the same evidence.
If students finish early, have them add a stronger method for collecting evidence.
If the room needs more structure, complete Part A together and release Parts B and C after a quick check.
When debriefing, praise cautious language and clear limits more than clever claims.
questions: What evidence would make the interpretation stronger?
What method could collect better evidence?
What does the evidence not show yet?
How could two different interpretations both fit the same observation?
look_fors: Student uses science language in the definition.
Student separates internal experience from observable action.
Student includes at least one limit.
Student avoids turning one example into a broad conclusion.

timing.activity: Launch the lesson with the four-word anchor: claim, evidence, method, limits.
Teach the working definition and compare internal experience with observable action.
Students complete task sheet Parts A and B. Pause for a quick check before moving on.
Students revise a weak interpretation using evidence, alternatives, and limits.
Students complete the exit response. Collect it as the lesson evidence artifact.

content.subtitle: The science of thought, feeling, and action.

content.scenario: A strong explanation starts with what we can observe and what evidence could support an interpretation.

content.model: Stronger explanations move in this order: claim, evidence, method, limits.
supports: Limits: What does this not prove?

bullets: Evidence: information gathered through observation or measurement.
Theory: a broad explanation supported over time.
Limit: what the evidence cannot show yet.

content.left: Weak: One action tells us the whole story.
content.right: Stronger: One action gives a starting point, but we need evidence, context, and limits.

prompts: Psychology is the scientific study of ___.
Name one internal example and one observable action example.
Write one limit for an interpretation.

rows: Psychology studies thought, feeling, and action scientifically.
Evidence is stronger than confident guessing.
One example is not enough for a broad conclusion.

task_sheet.title: What Is Psychology? — Student Task Sheet
task_sheet.purpose_line: Practise explaining action with evidence, method, and limits.
instructions: Include a limit before you finish.

tasks.label: Part A — Define the field
tasks.prompt: Write a clear definition of psychology using the words science, thought, feeling, and action.

tasks.label: Part B — Sort the examples
tasks.prompt: List one internal experience example and one observable action example from the class scenario.

tasks.label: Part C — Evidence before interpretation
tasks.prompt: What evidence would you need before making a stronger interpretation?

tasks.label: Part D — Improve the interpretation
tasks.prompt: Rewrite a weak interpretation so it includes evidence, at least one alternative, and one limit.

success_criteria: I separated internal experience from observable action.

exit_ticket.title: Exit Ticket — What Is Psychology?
exit_ticket.prompt: In 3-5 sentences, explain what psychology studies and why evidence and limits matter when interpreting human action.
